summary: Iris Murdoch argues that love, art, and morality are fundamentally connected by the difficult realization that something other than oneself is real. She posits that the 'fat relentless ego' obscures our view of reality, and that overcoming it requires a patient, loving attention to the particularity of others rather than a mere focus on moral actions.

principles.statement: Love is the discovery of reality.
principles.rationale: Love alerts us to the fact that something other than our own ego exists independently, allowing us to perceive the unutterable particularity of individuals.
principles.source_quote: Love is the extremely difficult realisation that something other than oneself is real. Love and so art and morals, is the discovery of reality.

principles.statement: Art and morals share the same essence.
principles.rationale: Both require us to overcome our selfish egos and pay loving attention to the reality of other individuals.
principles.source_quote: Art and morals are, with certain provisos… one. Their essence is the same. The essence of both of them is love.

principles.statement: Morality is about continuous attention, not just explicit choices.
principles.rationale: The moral life is ongoing and requires a just and loving gaze directed upon individual reality, rather than just switching on during moments of decision.
principles.source_quote: to express the idea of a just and loving gaze directed upon an individual reality. I believe this to be the characteristic and proper mark of the active moral agent

principles.statement: The enemy of morality is the ego.
principles.rationale: The ego obscures reality by attempting to assimilate the 'other' into an egotistical fantasy world.
principles.source_quote: the enemy is the fat relentless ego

frameworks.name: Refocusing Attention
frameworks.when_to_apply: When trying to overcome strong negative emotions (hatred, resentment, jealousy) or unwanted love, where 'pure will' fails.
steps: Recognize that pure willpower cannot simply stop a strong emotion.
Allow this refocusing to provide energy of a different kind from a different source.
frameworks.source_quote: Deliberately falling out of love is not a jump of the will, it is the acquiring of new objects of attention and thus of new energies as a result of refocusing

mental_models.name: The Patient Eye of Love
mental_models.description: Viewing moral life not as a series of discrete actions or choices, but as a continuous, just, and loving gaze directed upon individual reality.
mental_models.example: Observing other people with an 'undramatic, because un-self-centred, agnosticism which goes with tolerance,' respecting their autonomy to make mistakes.

mental_models.name: The Fat Relentless Ego
mental_models.description: A lens for understanding human failure in art and morals. The ego acts as a distorting filter that assimilates the 'other' into a personal fantasy world, preventing the perception of true reality.
mental_models.example: Artists failing to respect the autonomy of their characters, or individuals stereotyping others to quickly sum them up.

heuristics: 'Act lovingly' translates to 'Act perfectly' better than 'Act rationally' does.
To understand other people is a task which does not come to an end.

signature_quotes.text: Love is the extremely difficult realisation that something other than oneself is real.
signature_quotes.context: Defining the essence of love and its connection to discovering reality.

signature_quotes.text: Art and morals are, with certain provisos… one. Their essence is the same. The essence of both of them is love.
signature_quotes.context: Connecting the disciplines of art and morality through the concept of love.

signature_quotes.text: Form is the temptation of love and its peril… to round off a situation, to sum up a character.
signature_quotes.context: Warning against the urge to stereotype or quickly sum up other people.

signature_quotes.text: the enemy is the fat relentless ego
signature_quotes.context: Identifying the primary obstacle to becoming morally better.

signature_quotes.text: Deliberately falling out of love is not a jump of the will, it is the acquiring of new objects of attention and thus of new energies as a result of refocusing
signature_quotes.context: Explaining how to overcome strong emotions when pure willpower fails.

signature_quotes.text: The idea of a really good man living in a private dream world seems unacceptable
signature_quotes.context: Emphasizing that true goodness requires engaging with reality, not retreating into the ego.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on 'pure will' to change strong emotions.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Willpower alone cannot stop feelings like resentment or unwanted love; one must instead reorient attention to new objects to acquire new energies.

anti_patterns.description: Imposing 'form' on life and other people.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It leads to stereotyping and a too-quick summing up of a character, failing to respect the autonomy and unutterable particularity of the individual.

anti_patterns.description: Treating moral life as only a series of explicit choices.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It neglects the foundational 'patient eye of love' and the continuous attention required to truly see individual reality between explicit choices.

anti_patterns.description: Assimilating the 'other' into an egotistical fantasy world (Neurosis).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It obscures reality and prevents the genuine perception of individuals, which is the essence of both good art and morality.
